speakerNotes.cover: Hook the room in the first 10 seconds. The tagline should be memorable — don't explain the product yet, just set the stage.
speakerNotes.problem: Make the problem visceral. Tell the story of a real user if possible. Quantify the pain: 'it costs $X/month' or 'takes X hours/week'.
speakerNotes.solution: Show the product as soon as possible. A live demo beats any slide. The analogy ('the X for Y') helps investors categorise you quickly.
speakerNotes.howItWorks: Keep it to 3 steps max. The goal is to show it's actionable, not to explain the technical architecture.
speakerNotes.businessModel: Investors want to know if the model is defensible and scalable. If you lack data, say so clearly and present your assumptions.
speakerNotes.market: Source your numbers. VCs typically want a SAM >$500M. Walk through the SOM logic: number of customers × ARPU.
speakerNotes.traction: Often the most important slide. Be honest — investors will verify. If traction is thin, highlight qualitative signals (LOIs, pilots, waitlists).
speakerNotes.gtm: Show you've tested channels and drawn lessons. 'We'll run ads' is not a GTM strategy.
speakerNotes.competition: Never ignore competitors — it kills credibility. Acknowledge their strengths, then show why your approach is different or better.
speakerNotes.team: Investors fund teams as much as ideas. Highlight why YOU are uniquely positioned to solve this problem.
speakerNotes.roadmap: The roadmap shows you can prioritise. Link each post-raise item to the use of funds on the next slide.
speakerNotes.ask: Be specific about use of funds. 'Grow the product' is not enough. Show what milestone this round unlocks and why now is the right time to raise.
